content.text: AI agents accomplish their goals by calling tools — functions that query databases, send messages, execute code, invoke APIs, or take real-world actions. The governance question is not just whether the agent has access to the right tools, but whether every tool in the agent's arsenal is documented, assessed for risk, authorized for the contexts where it is used, and tested for the ways it can go wrong. Tool capability datasheets are the governance mechanism that answers these questions systematically, creating a structured inventory of every tool available to AI agents in production.

A tool capability datasheet describes a single tool: its purpose, its input and output schema, its risk classification, the authorization requirements for invoking it, how it has been tested (including adversarial testing), and the process for updating it. Together, the datasheets for all tools available to an agent form the tool inventory that feeds into risk assessments, system cards, and audit evidence. Without tool-level documentation, the agent's system card describes capabilities at too high a level to be actionable for governance.

attrs.alt: Tool capability datasheet anatomy showing identity, purpose, inputs, outputs, risk level, authorization, testing, update process, and audit log fields, alongside T1-T4 risk tiers from read-only through irreversible/critical

content.text: Why Tool Datasheets Matter

When an AI agent causes an incident — sends an unauthorized email, deletes a database record, triggers an unintended financial transaction — the investigation invariably asks: what tools did the agent have access to, what authorization was required to invoke them, and what testing had been done to verify that the agent would not invoke them in harmful ways? If the organization cannot answer these questions from documentation, the post-incident explanation becomes 'we did not fully understand what the agent could do,' which is precisely the governance failure regulators are looking to identify.

Tool datasheets also serve a design function: when teams are building new agents, the tool inventory makes the risk implications of tool access decisions explicit. Granting a customer support agent access to a payment refund tool requires the team to document that access in the T3 or T4 tier, define the authorization workflow, and test for adversarial scenarios where an attacker might try to induce the agent to issue unauthorized refunds. Without a datasheets framework, these decisions are made informally and inconsistently.
Benefits
Creates a comprehensive tool inventory that feeds agent risk assessments and system cards with accurate tool access information.
Forces explicit risk classification decisions at the tool level — teams must justify granting access to T3 or T4 tools rather than treating all tools as equivalent.
Defines authorization requirements per tool, ensuring appropriate human oversight is built into high-risk tool invocations.
Enables scope-limited testing: testers know which tools require adversarial testing and which require only functional testing based on the risk tier.
Satisfies audit evidence requirements: when auditors ask what safeguards exist for agent tool use, the datasheet register is the primary evidence artifact.
Provides an operational reference for on-call engineers responding to tool-related incidents.
Trade-offs
Maintaining datasheets for a large tool library is overhead that engineering teams may resist without compliance program mandate.
Tool schemas evolve with APIs — datasheets that are not updated after API changes become inaccurate and may mislead governance reviews.
Risk tier assignments are judgment calls that can be inconsistent across teams without a clear rubric and review process.
Datasheets for proprietary third-party tools may contain vendor information that cannot be publicly shared, complicating audit evidence disclosure.
Risk Tier Classification

Not all tools carry the same risk. A read-only web search tool and a financial settlement tool are both 'tools' in an agent framework, but the governance requirements are entirely different. Risk tiers provide a structured approach to differentiation.
T1: Read-Only / Lookup

T1 tools retrieve information but make no state changes to any system. Examples: web search, document retrieval, database read queries, status checks, weather APIs, knowledge base lookup. Governance requirements: functional testing for correctness; schema validation; output sanitization to prevent injection attacks via retrieved content. Authorization: generally any agent with legitimate access to the data source may invoke T1 tools without additional approval workflow.
T2: Write / Create (Reversible)

T2 tools create or modify state in a reversible way. Examples: creating a support ticket, writing a file to a staging location, appending to a log, scheduling a meeting invitation. Governance requirements: functional and adversarial testing; confirmation that the write scope is limited to the minimum necessary; rollback procedure documented. Authorization: approval workflow recommended; human review of agent behavior for the first N invocations after tool deployment.
T3: External / Financial (Hard to Reverse)

T3 tools have external effects that are difficult to undo. Examples: sending an email or Slack message, triggering a webhook to a third-party service, initiating a payment that requires a reversal process, posting to a social media account. Governance requirements: human approval before invocation or strict confirmation flow with the user; rate limiting; comprehensive adversarial testing; immediate alerting on unusual invocation patterns. Authorization: human approval required, or explicit user confirmation at invocation time.
T4: Irreversible / Critical

T4 tools take actions that cannot be undone or that have high-consequence irreversible effects. Examples: deleting a production database record, deploying code to production, issuing a financial settlement, sending a legally binding document. Governance requirements: multi-party approval (at least two authorized humans must approve); MFA at invocation; complete audit trail of the approval chain; post-invocation notification to named stakeholders; access to T4 tools must be separately justified and periodically re-reviewed. No AI agent should have autonomous access to T4 tools — human approval is mandatory.
Mental Model: Tools as Power Tools

Think of agent tools the way a construction site thinks of power tools. A tape measure (T1: read-only) can be used by anyone on-site without special authorization. A drill (T2: write, reversible) requires basic training and the right drill bit for the material. A nail gun (T3: external, hard to undo) requires training, protective equipment, and a supervisor nearby. A demolition saw (T4: irreversible, high-consequence) requires multiple authorizations, a permit, and two qualified operators who both verify the cut location before the saw runs. The tool capability datasheet is the equivalent of the tool authorization card that documents which tools can be used where, by whom, and with what safeguards.
Datasheet Structure
Tool Identity: unique name, version, owner team, risk tier, last review date.
Purpose: one-paragraph description of what the tool does and what business need it serves.
Input Schema: parameter names, types, required vs optional, validation rules, size limits.
Output Schema: return value structure, error types, null handling, latency characteristics.
Risk Classification: tier (T1-T4) with explicit rationale; consequences of misuse; data sensitivity of inputs and outputs.
Authorization Requirements: who may invoke the tool; whether human approval is required; MFA requirements; rate limits.
Testing Methodology: functional test coverage; adversarial test scenarios; test results summary; known gaps.
Audit Logging: what is logged at invocation; log retention period; PII handling in logs; where logs are stored.
Update Process: who must approve schema or behavior changes; how version increments are handled; notification requirements for downstream agents.
Known Issues: any known defects, rate limit behaviors, or failure modes from production experience.
Code Example: Tool Datasheet Schema and Registry

"""
tool_datasheet.py
Structured schema for tool capability datasheets.
Provides a registry to store and query tool documentation.
"""

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import date
from typing import Optional, Literal
import json

RiskTier = Literal["T1", "T2", "T3", "T4"]
ParameterType = Literal["string", "integer", "float", "boolean", "array", "object"]

@dataclass
class ParameterSpec:
    name: str
    type: ParameterType
    required: bool
    description: str
    constraints: Optional[str] = None   # e.g., "max_length: 500", "min: 0, max: 100"
    example: Optional[str] = None

@dataclass
class TestResult:
    test_type: Literal["functional", "adversarial", "performance", "access_control"]
    test_date: date
    outcome: Literal["pass", "partial", "fail"]
    notes: str

@dataclass
class ToolDatasheet:
    # Identity
    tool_id: str
    tool_name: str
    version: str
    owner_team: str
    risk_tier: RiskTier
    last_reviewed: date
    next_review: date

    # Purpose
    purpose: str
    use_cases: list[str]
    not_intended_for: list[str]

    # Input / Output
    input_parameters: list[ParameterSpec]
    output_schema: str     # JSON schema reference or inline description
    error_types: list[str]
    latency_p95_ms: int
    reliability_sla: str   # e.g., "99.5% success rate over 30 days"

    # Risk
    risk_rationale: str
    consequences_of_misuse: str
    data_sensitivity: Literal["none", "internal", "confidential", "restricted"]

    # Authorization
    authorized_agents: list[str]     # agent IDs or "all_internal"
    human_approval_required: bool
    mfa_required: bool
    rate_limit: Optional[str]        # e.g., "100 calls/hour per agent"

    # Testing
    test_results: list[TestResult]
    adversarial_scenarios_tested: list[str]
    known_gaps: list[str]

    # Audit
    invocation_logged: bool
    log_destination: str
    log_retention_days: int
    pii_in_logs: bool
    pii_handling: Optional[str] = None

    # Update process
    update_approval_required_from: list[str]
    breaking_change_notification: str

    def risk_summary(self) -> dict:
        return {
            "tool_id": self.tool_id,
            "tool_name": self.tool_name,
            "risk_tier": self.risk_tier,
            "human_approval_required": self.human_approval_required,
            "data_sensitivity": self.data_sensitivity,
            "adversarial_scenarios_tested": len(self.adversarial_scenarios_tested),
            "known_gaps": len(self.known_gaps),
        }

# Example: Email Send tool (T3)
email_tool = ToolDatasheet(
    tool_id="TOOL-EMAIL-SEND-v2",
    tool_name="send_email",
    version="2.1.0",
    owner_team="Communications Platform Team",
    risk_tier="T3",
    last_reviewed=date(2026, 7, 1),
    next_review=date(2026, 10, 1),
    purpose="Send transactional emails on behalf of the AI agent to customers or internal recipients",
    use_cases=["Send order confirmation", "Notify customer of support ticket update", "Send account alert"],
    not_intended_for=["Mass marketing emails", "Legal notices", "Emails to external regulators"],
    input_parameters=[
        ParameterSpec("to", "string", True, "Recipient email address", "valid RFC 5322 email format"),
        ParameterSpec("subject", "string", True, "Email subject line", "max_length: 150"),
        ParameterSpec("body", "string", True, "Plain text or HTML email body", "max_length: 50000"),
        ParameterSpec("cc", "array", False, "CC recipients", "max 5 addresses"),
        ParameterSpec("template_id", "string", False, "Pre-approved template ID", None),
    ],
    output_schema='{"message_id": "string", "status": "sent|queued|failed", "timestamp": "ISO8601"}',
    error_types=["INVALID_RECIPIENT", "RATE_LIMIT_EXCEEDED", "TEMPLATE_NOT_FOUND", "SEND_FAILED"],
    latency_p95_ms=800,
    reliability_sla="99.5% success rate (30-day rolling)",
    risk_rationale="External communication tool with irreversible external effects; misuse could damage customer relationships, violate CAN-SPAM, or be exploited for phishing via prompt injection",
    consequences_of_misuse="Regulatory violation (CAN-SPAM, GDPR), customer harm, reputational damage, account suspension",
    data_sensitivity="confidential",
    authorized_agents=["customer-support-chatbot-prod", "order-confirmation-agent-prod"],
    human_approval_required=True,
    mfa_required=False,
    rate_limit="50 emails/hour per agent; 500/day",
    test_results=[
        TestResult("functional", date(2026, 6, 15), "pass", "All 50 functional scenarios pass"),
        TestResult("adversarial", date(2026, 6, 20), "pass", "Prompt injection attempts to send to non-customer addresses blocked by recipient allowlist"),
        TestResult("access_control", date(2026, 6, 20), "pass", "Unauthorized agent invocation returns 403"),
    ],
    adversarial_scenarios_tested=[
        "Prompt injection via customer input to change recipient address",
        "Attempt to send to disallowed domain (competitor, regulator)",
        "Oversized attachment injection",
        "HTML injection in body to execute malicious content",
    ],
    known_gaps=["Rate limit enforcement is per-agent, not per-user-session; a compromised session could exhaust the rate limit"],
    invocation_logged=True,
    log_destination="Splunk: ai-tool-audit-logs",
    log_retention_days=365,
    pii_in_logs=True,
    pii_handling="Recipient email masked to first 3 chars + domain in logs; body content hashed",
    update_approval_required_from=["AI Platform Lead", "Legal (for policy changes)", "Security (for auth changes)"],
    breaking_change_notification="All authorized agents must be notified 14 days before breaking schema changes",
)

print(json.dumps(email_tool.risk_summary(), indent=2))
Code Example: YAML Datasheet Template

# tool-capability-datasheet.yaml
# Template for documenting AI agent tool capabilities.
# One file per tool; version-controlled alongside tool implementation.

tool_identity:
  tool_id: TOOL-DB-WRITE-v1
  tool_name: write_customer_record
  version: 1.3.0
  owner_team: Data Platform Team
  risk_tier: T2  # Write / Create (reversible)
  last_reviewed: 2026-07-01
  next_review: 2026-10-01

purpose: |
  Writes or updates customer profile records in the production CRM database.
  Used by AI agents to capture information gathered during customer interactions.

use_cases:
  - Update customer contact preferences after support interaction
  - Record new shipping address provided by customer
  - Log consent status after AI-presented consent flow

not_intended_for:
  - Mass data imports
  - Deleting records (use TOOL-DB-DELETE for that — T4 tier)
  - Writing to tables outside the customer_profiles schema

input_parameters:
  customer_id:
    type: string
    required: true
    description: Unique customer identifier (UUID format)
    constraints: "must match existing record; tool will not create new records"
  fields_to_update:
    type: object
    required: true
    description: Key-value pairs of fields to update
    constraints: "only fields in ALLOWED_FIELDS list accepted"
    allowed_fields:
      - contact_preferences
      - shipping_address
      - communication_consent
      - profile_notes

output_schema:
  success:
    updated_fields: [list of field names written]
    version: integer (record version after write)
    timestamp: ISO8601
  error:
    code: [RECORD_NOT_FOUND, FIELD_NOT_ALLOWED, VALIDATION_ERROR, DB_TIMEOUT]
    message: string

risk:
  tier: T2
  rationale: "Writes are reversible via version history; scope limited to non-sensitive fields"
  consequences_of_misuse: "Incorrect customer data could cause service failures; mass update could corrupt CRM data"
  data_sensitivity: confidential

authorization:
  authorized_agents:
    - customer-support-chatbot-prod
    - onboarding-agent-prod
  human_approval_required: false
  mfa_required: false
  rate_limit: "1000 writes/hour per agent"
  field_allowlist_enforced: true

testing:
  functional:
    status: pass
    last_run: 2026-06-30
    coverage: 95%
  adversarial:
    status: pass
    scenarios_tested:
      - "Attempt to write to non-allowed field (expect rejection)"
      - "Attempt to write to non-existent customer_id (expect RECORD_NOT_FOUND)"
      - "SQL injection in field value (expect sanitization)"
    last_run: 2026-06-30
  known_gaps:
    - "Rate limit is not enforced at DB level; only enforced by gateway"

audit_logging:
  logged: true
  destination: "Splunk: ai-tool-audit-logs"
  retention_days: 365
  pii_in_logs: true
  pii_handling: "customer_id logged; field values logged with PII fields masked"

update_process:
  breaking_change_notice_days: 14
  approval_required_from:
    - Data Platform Lead
    - Security Review
  version_increment_policy: "patch for bug fixes, minor for new allowed fields, major for breaking schema changes"
Real-World Example: Financial Services Tool Governance

A wealth management firm used AI agents for client communication and portfolio reporting. One agent had access to a tool that could send client communications including performance reports. When an audit reviewed the agent's tool access, the auditor asked for documentation showing that the email tool had been tested for prompt injection scenarios where adversarial client messages could be used to redirect the agent to send unauthorized communications. The team had functional test results but no adversarial test documentation. The auditor rated this a P2 finding. Subsequently, the firm implemented tool capability datasheets for all tools with T3 or T4 risk classification, including mandatory adversarial test documentation as a prerequisite for tool authorization. The next audit cycle found the datasheets and adversarial test records in order and the finding was closed.
Common Mistakes
Classifying all tools as T1 or T2 to avoid the governance overhead of T3/T4 classification — underclassification defeats the purpose of the tiering system.
Writing datasheets only for the tools the team thinks are risky, not for all tools — the tool inventory must be comprehensive to be a reliable governance artifact.
Treating adversarial testing as optional for low-risk tools — even T1 tools can be exploited through output injection; testing scope varies by tier but is never zero.
Updating the tool implementation without updating the datasheet — tool versions must be synchronized with datasheet versions.
Not specifying a field allowlist for write tools — open-ended write tools with no field constraints are inherently higher risk than documented.
Best Practices
Require a completed datasheet as a gate for tool inclusion in any agent's tool set — no datasheet, no deployment.
Maintain a tool registry that maps each tool to the agents that have access to it, making tool-to-agent relationships queryable for incident investigation.
Use automated schema validation to verify that the production tool API still matches the datasheet's input/output specification — schema drift is a common source of governance failures.
Conduct adversarial testing for every T3 and T4 tool before initial deployment and after any behavior-changing update.
Review T3 and T4 tool authorization lists quarterly — agent access to high-risk tools must be re-justified regularly.
Store datasheets in version control with the tool implementation so that the governance artifact and the code are always in sync.
Performance and Scalability

Organizations with large tool libraries (20+ tools across multiple agents) should invest in a tool registry system that provides: searchable inventory of all tools; current datasheet for each tool; agent-to-tool access mapping; alert notifications when a tool's datasheet is due for review. This can be as simple as a well-structured Git repository with a registry index file, or as sophisticated as a dedicated tool governance module in a GRC platform.
Comparison with Alternatives
Approach
Coverage
Governance Value
Effort
Incident Support
Tool capability datasheets (this)
All tools documented
High
Medium per tool
High — structured evidence
Code comments only
Implementation only
Low

Very low
Tool README files

Low
Low
Low
System card (agent level)

Medium

Medium
High
Medium
Security and Reliability

The tool datasheet register is itself a security-sensitive document: it reveals which tools are available to which agents, the authorization bypass conditions, and known gaps in adversarial testing. Access to the full internal register should be restricted to the AI governance team, security team, and named auditors. For customer-facing documentation, tool capabilities should be described at the system card level (the agent has email-sending capability) rather than at the datasheet level (here is the exact API schema for the email tool).
Key Takeaways
Tool capability datasheets document individual tools available to AI agents: purpose, inputs, outputs, risk tier, authorization requirements, test results, and audit logging.
The T1-T4 risk tier system classifies tools from read-only lookup (T1) through irreversible/critical actions (T4), with escalating authorization and testing requirements at each tier.
No AI agent should have autonomous access to T4 tools — multi-party human approval is mandatory regardless of agent capability or prior track record.
Adversarial testing is required for T3 and T4 tools before deployment; the test results must be documented in the datasheet and updated after any behavior-changing tool update.
The tool registry (index of all datasheets) is the primary evidence artifact when auditors ask what tools agents have access to and how they are governed.
Datasheets must be versioned alongside tool implementations — a datasheet that describes an older API version is a governance gap, not evidence of control.
The tool-to-agent mapping in the registry enables rapid scope assessment during incident investigation, showing exactly which agents could have invoked a specific tool.
